question:
Patients receiving chemotherapy as outpatients were randomized to receive either an active antiemetic treatment or placebo (Williams et al., 1989). The following table shows measurements (in mm ) on a 100 mm linear analogue self-assessment scale for nausea.

 egin{tabular}{lc}
\hline \multicolumn{2}{l}{ Treatment group } \
Active & Placebo \
$(n=20)$ & $(n=20)$ \
\hline 0 & 0 \
0 & 10 \
0 & 12 \
0 & 15 \
0 & 15 \
2 & 30 \
7 & 35 \
8 & 38 \
10 & 42 \
13 & 45 \
\hline
\end{tabular}

 egin{tabular}{ll}
\hline \multicolumn{2}{l}{ Treatment group } \
Active & Placebo \
$(n=20)$ & $(n=20)$\
15 & 50 \
18 & 50 \
20 & 60 \
20 & 64 \
21 & 68 \
22 & 71 \
25 & 74 \
30 & 82 \
52 & 86 \
76 & 95 \
\hline
\end{tabular}

Identify and carry out an appropriate analysis to compare the values in the two groups.
answer:
The linear analogue scale data do not meet the distributional assumptions for parametric methods based on the $t$ distribution. The groups can be compared using the non-parametric Mann-Whitney test, which gives $z=3.29, \mathrm{P}=0.001$. There is thus strong evidence that nausea was less severe in patients receiving the active treatment.
A $95 \%$ confidence interval for the difference in median scores can be obtained using the method described by Campbell and Gardner (1989), and is 15 to 49 mm . In general estimates and confidence intervals are of limited value for data like these as the measurements have no straightforward interpretation.
